notes: Attacker directly injects goal-altering instructions into agent input
Hidden instructions in documents, emails, RAG results, or tool outputs alter agent goals without user visibility
Override safety guardrails that constrain agent goal execution
Organisational policy defines permissible agent autonomy — agents cannot change their stated goals without human confirmation
Agent goal hijack categorised in AI risk register per deployment — specific injection vectors mapped
Adversarial testing programme covers goal hijack scenarios — indirect injection via all agent data sources
Incident response for detected goal hijack — suspension procedure, action reversal checklist, OT impact assessment
Crafting inputs that produce tool calls with destructive parameters
Exploiting overly permissive tool access to exceed intended agent scope
Agent autonomously harvesting data through tool chains beyond authorised scope
Policy defines permissible tool invocations per agent role — human confirmation required for irreversible tools
All agent tool integrations mapped — data received, permission scope, reversibility, and risk rating per tool
Adversarial testing of tool misuse scenarios — destructive parameter injection, tool chaining, MCP descriptor poisoning
Incident response for tool misuse — tool disable, parameter audit, downstream impact assessment
Exploiting legitimate agent credentials to access AI systems or data pipelines
Using compromised agent credentials to exfiltrate data through inference API
Reconstructing sensitive data accessible to the agent through credential abuse
Agent identity and credential governance policy — NHI inventory, short-lived credentials, scope controls
Agent credential exposure risks mapped per deployment — credential types, scope, and lateral movement potential
Adversarial testing covering credential leakage and abuse — memory stores, logs, tool payload captures
Incident response for credential exposure — rotation, containment, lateral movement assessment, downstream notification
Tampering with model weights or tool components loaded by agent at runtime
Persistent malicious behaviour introduced through dynamically loaded agent components
Trigger-based backdoors in MCP servers, prompt templates, or model adapters
Supply chain governance policy — approved sources for agent tools, MCP servers, and model components
All agent supply chain components mapped — dynamic tool loading inventoried, approved before use
Supply chain integrity testing — signature verification, descriptor review, backdoor scanning
Residual supply chain risk documented and treated — third-party component risks in AI risk register
Agent-generated code or payloads executed by downstream components
Overriding code execution safety guardrails to allow arbitrary command execution
Crafting inputs that produce malicious executable code in agent output
Policy explicitly addresses agent code execution capability — sandbox requirements, permitted operations
Code execution risk categorised separately per agent — blast radius, permitted operations, sandbox status
Adversarial testing of code execution paths — sandbox escape attempts, command injection via generated code
Incident response for agent code execution anomaly — sandbox isolation, kill switch, forensic capture
Injecting malicious content into agent persistent memory or RAG stores
Crafting content whose embeddings bias future retrieval results in attacker's favour
Agent memory treated as sensitive data asset — classification, access controls, retention policy
Memory poisoning risk mapped per agent — memory stores, trust levels, access controls, TTL documented
Adversarial testing of memory integrity — poisoning scenarios, anomaly detection effectiveness
Response for confirmed memory poisoning — audit procedure, content purge, operational impact assessment
Identifying and mapping inter-agent communication endpoints for targeting
Using compromised agent credentials to impersonate trusted agents in A2A channels
Intercepting inter-agent messages to exfiltrate sensitive context passed between agents
A2A communication security policy — authentication, encryption, schema validation requirements
All A2A communication channels mapped — authentication method, encryption status, schema validation, logging
A2A security testing — spoofing, replay, man-in-the-middle scenarios on all inter-agent channels
Incident response for A2A compromise — channel isolation, agent suspension, forensic capture
Triggering cascading failure propagation to exhaust system resources or degrade service
Crafting inputs that trigger runaway agent loops generating unbounded costs
Exploiting an exposed agent endpoint to introduce a fault that cascades internally
Cascade blast radius defined and accepted per deployment — maximum affected systems documented
Cascade resilience testing — circuit breaker effectiveness, failover scenarios, chaos engineering
Incident response for cascade events — defined suspension procedure, process control fallback
Residual cascade risk documented — BCP coverage for agent cluster failures
Agent produces high-volume, fluent content that overwhelms human critical assessment
Compromised agent crafts highly personalised, convincing manipulation targeted at specific users
Policy on agent transparency — agents must identify as AI, advisory outputs clearly distinguished from authoritative
Evaluation of transparency controls — verify advisory labels persist in all interface contexts
Feedback channels detecting operator over-trust patterns — aggregate analysis of agent-influenced decisions
Response for detected trust exploitation patterns — operator retraining, interface redesign, aggregate audit
Persistent override of agent safety constraints enabling hidden goal execution
Rogue agent gradually escalates its own capabilities and permissions over time
Compromised agent maintains persistent access and hidden execution across sessions
Policy requires behavioural monitoring of all deployed agents — rogue agent detection as a governance objective
Rogue agent risk categorised per deployment — detection capability, blast radius, and response procedures mapped
Adversarial testing covering rogue agent scenarios — behavioural drift, persistent hidden goal detection
Incident response for rogue agent — kill switch, audit procedure, operational impact assessment, root cause
Attacker directly manipulates user-facing prompt to alter model behaviour
Hidden instructions in content the model processes (documents, web, RAG)
Circumventing model safety guardrails via crafted prompt sequences
Organisational policies explicitly address adversarial input risks including prompt injection
Prompt injection categorised as a high-priority risk in the AI system risk register
Adversarial testing programme validates model resilience to prompt injection at each release
Defined incident response procedures for detected prompt injection attacks

Foundational
Unintended exposure of training data or sensitive context through model outputs
Reconstructing training data from model outputs or confidence scores
Organisational policies for data privacy in AI systems — classification, handling, and disclosure controls
Sensitive information disclosure categorised and risk-rated in AI system risk register
Evaluation programme includes data leakage and privacy disclosure testing
Defined procedures for responding to sensitive data disclosure incidents
Foundational
Foundational
Foundational
Tampering with model weights, adapters, or configurations during supply chain
Introducing persistent malicious behaviour into model through supply chain
Embedding trigger-based backdoors in model weights via training or fine-tuning supply chain
Supply chain data governance — policies for third-party model and data provenance
Mapping of all AI system dependencies including third-party models, datasets, and libraries
Evaluation programme includes supply chain component integrity testing
Residual risk from third-party components documented, monitored, and treated
Foundational

Hardening
Foundational
Injecting malicious data into training pipelines to corrupt model behaviour
Embedding hidden trigger-response patterns in model via poisoned training data
Data and model poisoning categorised as Critical in AI risk register
Adversarial testing specifically covering poisoning detection in training pipelines
Data quality measurement and validation applied to all training and fine-tuning data
Defined response procedures including model rollback for detected poisoning events
Foundational
Foundational

Foundational
Crafting inputs that produce dangerous outputs consumed by downstream systems
LLM outputs containing serialised payloads executed by downstream components
Output handling security included in AI evaluation and testing programme
Output leakage and injection scenarios covered in evaluation
Incident response for output handling failures defined and tested
Organisational policy requires secure output handling for all LLM integrations

Exploiting overly permissive LLM tool access to perform actions beyond intended scope
LLM autonomously collecting data beyond its intended access scope
Organisational policy defines and enforces limits on AI system autonomy and decision authority
Excessive agency risks mapped to specific tool and API integrations in the risk register
Autonomy boundary testing included in adversarial evaluation programme
Defined response for unauthorised autonomous actions including rollback and notification

Extraction of internal model configuration, instructions, or system prompts
Crafting inputs specifically designed to reveal or override system prompt content
System prompts classified as sensitive configuration — subject to data governance policy
Prompt extraction scenarios included in evaluation programme
Defined response for detected system prompt leakage
System prompt leakage risk mapped and rated in AI risk register

Crafting inputs whose embeddings manipulate similarity search results
Flooding vector stores with adversarial embeddings to degrade retrieval quality
Vector store and embedding adversarial testing included in evaluation programme
Data quality controls applied to embedding generation and vector store ingestion
Response procedures for detected vector store compromise or manipulation
Embedding and vector store risks mapped in AI system risk register

Foundational
Generating high-volume automated content to shape perception or overwhelm fact-checking
Organisational policy on AI-generated content accuracy and human oversight requirements
Accuracy and hallucination testing included in AI evaluation programme
Feedback channels for detecting and tracking misinformation in production
Procedures for responding to detected misinformation incidents

Foundational
Overloading AI systems with computationally expensive inputs to cause service degradation
Crafting inputs that maximise token usage or API costs per request
Resource exhaustion and denial-of-service scenarios included in adversarial testing
Defined response to detected unbounded consumption events including rate limiting activation
Residual availability risk from resource exhaustion documented and treated
Organisational policy defines resource limits and availability requirements for all LLM deployments
